episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Kottke.org] How to Make a Living as an Artist and [Boing Boing] Sci-Hub and Libgen descend from a Soviet tradition of smuggled science, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities like the Cariboo build sustainable digital archival infrastructure to preserve cultural memory, journalism, and Indigenous knowledge while ensuring long-term maintenance and authenticity? Topics include: Fragility of journalism and cultural archives in rural Canada and preservation gaps, Technical solutions: Mukurtu platform, TRACE provenance standard, digitization capacity, Organizational models: regional consortiums vs. independent institution efforts, Indigenous cultural sovereignty and copyright/governance questions in archival work, Sustainability problem: pilot-to-permanent transition failures and maintenance capacity gaps.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

discussion.central_question: How can rural communities like the Cariboo build sustainable digital archival infrastructure to preserve cultural memory, journalism, and Indigenous knowledge while ensuring long-term maintenance and authenticity?
discussion.riley_position: Regional institutions have the technical capacity and funding mechanisms to build shared digital archives using platforms like Mukurtu and TRACE standards; the barrier is organizational coordination and design of sustainable funding models, not technology.
riley_key_evidence: Transcription and OCR technology for old newsprint has improved substantially and is no longer the limiting factor
Council of Prairie and Pacific University Libraries demonstrates a successful regional consortium model for shared digital infrastructure
TRACE standard (tracestandard.org) provides cryptographically verifiable provenance chains for cultural material authenticity
discussion.casey_position: Regional pilots consistently fail due to the maintenance gap—grants end, metadata breaks, software becomes obsolete, and volunteer organizations lack capacity to sustain systems long-term; institutional capacity and legal/governance clarity remain unsolved.
casey_key_evidence: The common track record shows digitization projects end when grants expire, metadata deteriorates, and files become unreadable in ten years
Small cultural organizations in rural BC operate at capacity limits and lack dedicated IT staff, legal clarity on copyright, and stable funding comparable to university libraries
University library consortiums work because members have IT staff, legal resources, and institutional funding that rural arts societies don't have at comparable scale
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey agreed that while technical solutions exist and funding pathways are available, the real barriers are organizational and sustained resourcing. They identified Indigenous-led archival infrastructure as more promising than settler institutions due to clearer governance mandates and federal funding relationships, while acknowledging they lack direct consultation with those nations and inviting correction.
topics_covered: Fragility of journalism and cultural archives in rural Canada and preservation gaps
Technical solutions: Mukurtu platform, TRACE provenance standard, digitization capacity
Organizational models: regional consortiums vs. independent institution efforts
Indigenous cultural sovereignty and copyright/governance questions in archival work
Sustainability problem: pilot-to-permanent transition failures and maintenance capacity gaps
